meta.title: G87 Engine Replacement Cost UK — Compare Quotes
meta.description: Every BMW M2 G87 engine — the S58 3.0-litre twin-turbo straight-six. Real UK costs and honest performance car reliability data. Get free quotes from 20+ vetted BMW specialists today.

mainEntity.name: How much does a BMW M2 G87 engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned G87 S58 engine typically costs £5,000–£8,500 supply only [BMW-QUOTE], depending on model year — pre-LCI sits at £5,000–£8,000, while LCI can reach £8,500. Fitting adds £650–£1,300 depending on specialist location. The G87's strong retained values mean replacement is almost always viable.

mainEntity.name: Which BMW M2 G87 engine is the most reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The G87 is only offered with one engine family — the S58B30 3.0-litre twin-turbo straight-six — and it's a proven unit. Shared with the M3 and M4, the S58 has a strong reliability reputation with no widespread issues reported. The pre-LCI and LCI engines are mechanically identical [PROVISIONAL — pending dedicated Engine Page].

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the BMW M2 G87?

acceptedAnswer.text: The S58 engine is generally reliable, but cooling system maintenance is critical — the water pump and coolant hoses can fail, particularly on track-used cars. Oil leaks have been reported on some high-mileage examples. The manual transmission can exhibit clutch chatter. HPFP issues have been reported on early examples.

mainEntity.name: Is the BMW M2 G87 reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes, the G87 M2 is generally reliable. The S58 engine is an evolution of the proven S55 and shares parts from the reliable B58. It has been used in the X3 M, X4 M, M3, and M4 without widespread issues. However, as a high-performance engine, regular maintenance is essential.

mainEntity.name: Which BMW M2 G87 engine should I avoid?

acceptedAnswer.text: The G87 is only offered with one engine — the S58 — and it's a good one. There are no engine variants to avoid. The pre-LCI and LCI engines are mechanically identical; the power bump comes from tuning, not hardware changes. The only consideration is whether you want the extra 20 bhp of the LCI model.

mainEntity.name: How do I know if my G87 engine needs replacing?

acceptedAnswer.text: Cooling system issues are the most common engine concern — overheating or coolant loss should be investigated immediately. Long cranking on startup may indicate HPFP failure. Oil leaks under the car or a burning oil smell suggest valve cover or oil filter housing leaks.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a BMW M2 G87 engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on the car's value. Against a well-kept £35,000 G87, a £5,000–£8,500 S58 recon is 14–24% — well within our 70% BMW scrap threshold [BMW-VERIFIED]. The G87's strong retained values mean engine replacement is almost always a repair decision.

mainEntity.name: How long do BMW M2 G87 engines last?

acceptedAnswer.text: The S58 is a robust engine with a strong reliability reputation. With proper maintenance — regular oil changes, cooling system checks — it can exceed 100,000 miles. Track use will accelerate wear, particularly on cooling system components.

mainEntity.name: What is the difference between the G87 and the F87 M2?

acceptedAnswer.text: The G87 (2022–present) is the second-generation M2, built on BMW's CLAR platform with the S58 engine shared with the M3 and M4. The F87 (2015–2021) was the first-generation M2, built on the F22 platform with the N55 and S55 engines. The G87 is larger, more powerful, and the last pure ICE M car.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my BMW M2 G87 engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation; the engine code is also stamped on the engine block. The G87 M2 uses the S58B30 engine — pre-LCI (2022–2023) or S58B30T0 (LCI, 2024–present). Tell us your model year and we'll confirm it — 20+ vetted BMW specialists will confirm it for free.

confidenceScore.title: How confident are these ratings?
confidenceScore.text: The S58 engine is widely regarded as reliable — it is an evolution of the S55 and shares parts and configurations from the B58, another highly reliable performance motor. The engine has been used in various BMW M models, including the X3 M, X4 M, M3, and M4. However, as the G87 M2 was released in 2022, the engine has not had time to show any widespread reliability issues. The pre-LCI and LCI engines are mechanically similar; the power bump comes from revised engine tuning rather than hardware changes.

overview.intro: The G87 is the second generation of the BMW M2, unveiled on 11 October 2022 as the successor to the F87. Built on the CLAR platform shared with the G80 M3 and G82 M4, the G87 features a longer wheelbase, wider track, and a more aggressive design language — including the signature vertical kidney grille. Power comes exclusively from the S58 3.0-litre twin-turbo inline-six, detuned from the M3/M4 to 453 bhp in pre-LCI form and 473 bhp from the 2024 LCI onward. The G87 is rear-wheel drive only, with a six-speed manual transmission standard in some markets and an eight-speed automatic available. A significant 2024 LCI update brought more power, new colours, and iDrive 8.5 technology. The G87 is widely considered BMW's last purely internal combustion M car.
overview.keyFacts: Platform: BMW CLAR (shared with G80 M3, G82 M4) • Predecessor: F87 M2 • Successor: None (current generation) • Sibling body styles: G42 2 Series Coupé (base model).
overview.marketIntelligenceLine: The G87 M2 generated 120+ real UK engine enquiries in 2025 [BMW-VERIFIED], concentrated on the S58 engine as enthusiasts seek BMW's last pure ICE M car.

items.engine: S58B30 (3.0-litre twin-turbo straight-six) — the sole and exceptional engine.
items.quote: The BMW M2 G87 is offered with a single engine option — the S58B30 3.0-litre twin-turbo straight-six — and it's an exceptional one. Shared with the M3 and M4, the S58 produces 453 bhp and 550 Nm of torque in pre-LCI form, rising to 473 bhp and 600 Nm from the 2024 LCI onward. The engine is an evolution of the S55, known for being both powerful and dependable, and shares parts and configurations from the B58, another highly reliable performance motor. Power is sent to the rear wheels through either a six-speed manual or eight-speed automatic transmission, with 0-62 mph times of 4.3 seconds (manual) and 4.1 seconds (automatic). The S58 has a strong reliability reputation with no widespread issues reported, though cooling system maintenance (water pump, coolant hoses) and oil changes at 6,000–8,000 mile intervals rather than BMW's recommended 12,000 miles are recommended for track use.
items.whoItsFor: For buyers, there is no wrong choice — the S58 is a masterpiece of modern M engineering.

economicsRule.title: The G87 rule
economicsRule.text: A £5,000–£8,500 S58 recon against a £35,000–£55,000 G87 sits at 9–24% of the car's value【BMW-QUOTE】. The G87's strong retained values mean engine replacement is almost always a repair decision, not a financial one. For a near-new car under warranty, replacement is even less of a concern. The 2024 LCI cars command a premium, but the engine is mechanically similar to pre-LCI units.

keyTakeaways.question: Which G87 Costs Least To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: S58B30 — the only engine; running costs are reasonable for a 450 bhp performance car.

keyTakeaways.question: Which G87 Costs Most To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: S58B30T0 (LCI) — slight premium for the extra 20 bhp, but mechanically identical.

keyTakeaways.question: Which G87 Is Best Value?
keyTakeaways.answer: Pre-LCI (2022–2023) — lower entry price, same proven engine, and nearly identical performance.

problems.title: S58 Cooling System Maintenance
problems.rootCause: The S58 is a high-performance engine that generates significant heat. The water pump, coolant hoses, and expansion tank can fail with age and track use. Regular cooling system maintenance is essential for reliability.

tieredCosts.work: Water pump, thermostat, coolant hoses replacement

problems.recommendation: Have the cooling system inspected annually and replace the water pump as preventative maintenance at 60,000 miles or sooner if the car is used on track — a £500–£1,000 specialist job is dramatically cheaper than the £5,000+ engine replacement that follows an overheating event. This is the single most important maintenance item on any S58-powered BMW.

cta.label: Compare S58 engine prices for your G87 M2 →

problems.title: S58 Oil Leaks (Valve Cover, Oil Filter Housing)
problems.rootCause: The S58, like many high-performance BMW engines, can develop oil leaks from the valve cover gasket and oil filter housing. These are typically minor but can lead to more serious issues if ignored.

tieredCosts.work: Valve cover gasket and oil filter housing replacement

problems.recommendation: Have the valve cover and oil filter housing inspected during annual servicing — a £400–£800 repair is far cheaper than the £5,000+ engine replacement that follows if an oil leak leads to oil starvation. Check for oil stains under the car or a burning oil smell.

cta.label: Compare S58 engine prices for your G87 M2 →

problems.title: G87 M2 Manual Transmission Clutch Chatter
problems.affectedModels: G87 M2 with six-speed manual transmission.
problems.rootCause: Some G87 M2s with the six-speed manual transmission have experienced clutch chatter and binding issues — clunking on straight-line starts and binding in parking lots. This appears to be a characteristic of the clutch system rather than a mechanical defect, but it can be disconcerting.
problems.recommendation: If you experience clutch chatter on your manual G87 M2, have it inspected by a BMW specialist — in many cases, this is a characteristic of the clutch system rather than a fault. However, if the issue worsens, budget for a clutch replacement (£2,000–£3,500). The automatic transmission is smoother and quicker, which is why many owners prefer it.

problems.title: S58 High-Pressure Fuel Pump (HPFP) Issues
problems.affectedModels: All G87 M2 (S58 engine), particularly early (2022–2023) examples.
problems.rootCause: The S58's high-pressure fuel pump can fail — a known issue on BMW's B58/S58 family. Symptoms include long cranking, reduced power, and engine warning lights.
problems.recommendation: If you experience long cranking on startup or reduced power, have the HPFP inspected immediately — a £500–£1,000 replacement is far cheaper than the £5,000+ engine replacement that follows if ignored. This is a known issue on early S58-powered BMWs.

cta.label: Compare S58 HPFP prices for your G87 M2 →

coreVariants.scopeNote: The BMW M2 G87 is offered with a single engine option — the S58B30 twin-turbo straight-six. Pre-LCI models (2022–2023) produce 453 bhp; LCI models (2024–present) produce 473 bhp. Both are available with either a six-speed manual or eight-speed automatic transmission. The G87 is rear-wheel drive only; an xDrive model was introduced in Summer 2026. A limited-run M2 CS model was sold in 2025.

items.question: How much does a BMW M2 G87 engine replacement cost in the UK?
items.answer: A reconditioned G87 S58 engine typically costs £5,000–£8,500 supply only [BMW-QUOTE], depending on model year — pre-LCI (453 bhp) sits at £5,000–£8,000, while LCI (473 bhp) can reach £8,500. Fitting adds £650–£1,300 depending on specialist location. The G87's strong retained values (£35,000–£55,000+) mean engine replacement is almost always viable — at the top end, it's less than 25% of the car's value.

items.question: Which BMW M2 G87 engine is the most reliable?
items.answer: The G87 is only offered with one engine family — the S58B30 3.0-litre twin-turbo straight-six — and it's a proven unit. Shared with the M3 and M4, the S58 has a strong reliability reputation with no widespread issues reported. The pre-LCI (453 bhp) and LCI (473 bhp) engines are mechanically identical; the power bump comes from revised engine tuning [PROVISIONAL — pending dedicated Engine Page].

items.question: What are the common problems with the BMW M2 G87?
items.answer: The S58 engine is generally reliable, but cooling system maintenance is critical — the water pump and coolant hoses can fail, particularly on track-used cars. Oil leaks (valve cover gasket, oil filter housing) have been reported on some high-mileage examples. The manual transmission can exhibit clutch chatter, which is often a characteristic rather than a fault. HPFP issues have been reported on early (2022–2023) examples.

items.question: Is the BMW M2 G87 reliable?
items.answer: Yes, the G87 M2 is generally reliable. The S58 engine is an evolution of the proven S55 and shares parts from the reliable B58. It has been used in the X3 M, X4 M, M3, and M4 without widespread issues. However, as a high-performance engine, regular maintenance — particularly cooling system checks — is essential. The G87 is still a relatively new model, so long-term data is limited.

items.question: Which BMW M2 G87 engine should I avoid?
items.answer: The G87 is only offered with one engine — the S58 — and it's a good one. There are no engine variants to avoid. The pre-LCI and LCI engines are mechanically identical; the power bump comes from tuning, not hardware changes. The only consideration is whether you want the extra 20 bhp of the LCI model.

items.question: How do I know if my G87 engine needs replacing?
items.answer: Cooling system issues are the most common engine concern — overheating or coolant loss should be investigated immediately. Long cranking on startup may indicate HPFP failure. Oil leaks under the car or a burning oil smell suggest valve cover or oil filter housing leaks. Use our diagnostic tool for a personalised assessment.

items.question: Is it worth replacing a BMW M2 G87 engine, or should I scrap the car?
items.answer: Depends on the car's value. Against a well-kept £35,000 G87, a £5,000–£8,500 S58 recon is 14–24% of the car's value — well within our 70% BMW scrap threshold [BMW-VERIFIED]. The G87's strong retained values mean engine replacement is almost always a repair decision, not a financial one. For near-new cars, most owners will still be under warranty.

items.question: How long do BMW M2 G87 engines last?
items.answer: The S58 is a robust engine with a strong reliability reputation. With proper maintenance — regular oil changes, cooling system checks — it can exceed 100,000 miles. Track use will accelerate wear, particularly on cooling system components. Early examples (2022–2023) are still relatively low-mileage, so long-term data is limited, but the engine's proven architecture suggests strong durability.

items.question: What is the difference between the G87 and the F87 M2?
items.answer: The G87 (2022–present) is the second-generation M2, built on BMW's CLAR platform with the S58 engine shared with the M3 and M4. The F87 (2015–2021) was the first-generation M2, built on the F22 platform with the N55 (base) and S55 (Competition/CS) engines. The G87 is larger, more powerful, and features more advanced technology. The G87 is also the last purely internal combustion M car.

items.answer: Listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation; the engine code is also stamped on the engine block.
